intro.overview: Gulf Coast Healthcare Partners has a critical problem: their patient portal’s SSL certificate expired, and HIPAA auditors from the Office for Civil Rights (OCR) are arriving in six weeks. Protected Health Information (PHI) cannot travel over an unencrypted connection without triggering a reportable breach. Your job is to diagnose the certificate issue, remediate it properly, and document everything for the audit trail.

mentorQuote.title: Security Compliance Specialist, NEXUS Security
mentorQuote.quote: Before you touch anything, document the current state. Auditors want before-and-after evidence. Every screenshot tells a story — the story of due diligence, proper procedures, and professional remediation.

focusAreas.title: Certificate Lifecycle
focusAreas.description: X.509 certificate validity windows, expiration detection, and why expired certificates are a HIPAA compliance failure — not just a browser warning.

focusAreas.title: Certificate Signing Requests
focusAreas.description: Generating a CSR from an existing private key — the standard PKI workflow for renewal without regenerating keys or disrupting the trust chain.

focusAreas.title: CA Signing Operations
focusAreas.description: Using the Certificate Authority to sign a CSR and issue a new certificate with a 365-day validity window — the chain of trust in practice.

focusAreas.title: Before/After Evidence
focusAreas.description: Documenting the broken state before making changes — auditors require proof of the problem, not just proof of the fix.

focusAreas.title: Engagement Packet Compilation
focusAreas.description: Assembling three weeks of work into a single professional deliverable — the Section A/B/C structure used by NEXUS Security for all client engagements.

objectives: Deploy the Gulf Coast patient portal Docker container and diagnose the certificate issue
Generate a new Certificate Signing Request (CSR) and sign it with the existing CA
Install the new certificate and verify HTTPS functionality is restored
Compile all Module 2 work into a professional Engagement Packet

callouts.title: Required: DOCX Format
callouts.text: Submit as .docx (Microsoft Word) only. PDF submissions will not be accepted. Use Word’s built-in features for Table of Contents, page numbers, and citations.

callouts.title: Minimum Requirements
callouts.body: Your Engagement Packet must include: all 12 required sections in order, 25+ screenshots (9 from Lab 2.1, 8 from Lab 2.2, 8+ from Lab 2.3), VM hostname visible in every screenshot, and a minimum of 10 references. Man pages, RFCs, and textbook chapters all count.

clientContext.description: A regional healthcare network headquartered in Houston, Texas. Two years ago, they suffered a ransomware attack that forced a complete infrastructure rebuild.

contacts.name: Jennifer Walsh
contacts.title: CISO
contacts.quote: The OCR audit is in 6 weeks. I need this portal working and documented before they arrive.

contacts.name: Thomas Kowalski
contacts.title: VP Infrastructure
contacts.quote: I set this up five years ago when we were half this size. I know it needs updating.

contacts.name: Angela Washington
contacts.title: Privacy Officer
contacts.quote: If PHI travels over that portal unencrypted, we have a reportable breach.

youAlreadyHave.intro: This is NOT starting from scratch. You’ve been building this report for 3 weeks.

sections.title: From Week 1 — Lab 2.1: Cryptographic Foundations
items: Hash function demonstrations (SHA-256 comparison showing avalanche effect)
Symmetric encryption with AES-256-CBC (encrypt and decrypt cycle)
Asymmetric encryption with RSA keypairs
Digital signature creation and verification
9 screenshots (01-hash-sha256.png through 09-signature-verify.png)

sections.title: From Week 2 — Lab 2.2: Authentication Systems
items: SSH key generation — RSA and ED25519 comparison
File permission security demonstration (break/fix cycle)
SSH authorized_keys and config file setup
Multi-factor authentication configuration and verification
8 screenshots (10-keygen-rsa.png through 17-mfa-login.png)

sections.title: New This Week — Lab 2.3
items: Title Page, Table of Contents, Executive Summary
Certificate diagnosis and remediation documentation
Risk analysis and HIPAA compliance recommendations
Professional Reflection, References, Appendix

callouts.title: You're Already 60–70% Done
callouts.body: If you followed the Week 1–2 instructions, most of your report is already written. This week is about compilation and analysis, not starting over. Import your existing documentation, add the new analysis sections, and polish for submission.

callouts.title: You’re Already 60–70% Done
callouts.text: If you followed the Week 1–2 instructions, most of your report is already written. This week is about compilation and analysis, not starting over. Import your existing documentation, add the new analysis sections, and polish for submission.

timeGuide.timesheetNote: Week 3 is your first client-billable week. Checkpoint 1–3 time is billable to Gulf Coast Healthcare Partners. Checkpoint 4 (Engagement Packet compilation) is billable time as well — document it in Section A of your NEXUS Timesheet. Weeks 1 and 2 were internal training and are NOT client billable.

content.whatYoureDoing: Deploying the Gulf Coast patient portal container and documenting the broken state before making any changes.
content.whyItMatters: HIPAA requires documentation of security incidents and remediation. “Before” evidence proves due diligence for auditors. If you fix it first and document after, you have no proof of what the original problem was.

content.part: Part A: Deploy the Gulf Coast portal container

steps.text: Create a working directory for this engagement:
Change into your working directory:
Pull and start the Gulf Coast patient portal container:

whatThisDoes.concept: This downloads and starts a Docker container simulating Gulf Coast’s patient portal — an Apache web server with a deliberately expired SSL certificate.

flags.description: Map HTTPS port from container to host
Map SSH to port 2222 so it doesn’t conflict with your Kali SSH service
Assign a friendly name for later docker commands
The Docker Hub image to pull

content.part: Part B: Document the broken state

steps.text: Open Firefox and navigate to https://localhost — you should see a certificate warning. This is your “before” evidence.

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the browser certificate error. Save as gulfcoast-01-initial-error.png

steps.text: Check the certificate dates from your Kali terminal:

whatThisDoes.concept: This connects to the HTTPS server like a browser would, grabs the certificate, and displays its validity dates. The notBefore and notAfter dates tell you when the certificate was issued and when it expired.
whatThisDoes.output: The notAfter date should be in the past, confirming the certificate has expired.

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the openssl output showing the expired certificate dates. Save as gulfcoast-02-cert-expired.png

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Port conflict: Stop other containers using ports 443 or 2222 before running this command
Docker not running: If you get a “permission denied” error, make sure Docker is running and try adding sudo
Take screenshots NOW: You cannot recreate “before” evidence after fixing the certificate

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: Gulf Coast portal container running and accessible. Screenshots documenting the initial broken state: browser warning and expired certificate dates from openssl. These “before” screenshots are critical audit evidence.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 7 covers certificate lifecycles and expiration. The browser warning you document represents a real compliance failure — HIPAA requires valid certificates for protected health information.

content.title: Dig Deeper
content.text: What is a self-signed certificate and when is it appropriate to use one? Research the trust implications of self-signed vs. CA-signed certificates.

content.title: That’s a Reference!
content.text: Chapter 7 discusses certificate types and PKI hierarchy. Let’s Encrypt documentation explains the value of CA-signed certificates: https://letsencrypt.org/docs/

content.whatYoureDoing: Generating a new CSR using the existing private key, signing it with the CA, and installing the new certificate.
content.whyItMatters: Certificate renewal is one of the most common PKI operations you’ll perform. Understanding the CSR workflow — using the existing private key to generate a new request, then signing with the CA — ensures continuity without regenerating keys.

content.title: Security Compliance Specialist, NEXUS Security
content.quote: The README in the PKI directory is your roadmap. Real infrastructure always has documentation — sometimes outdated, sometimes incomplete — but always your starting point. Read it before you touch anything.

content.part: Part A: SSH into the container and explore the PKI structure

steps.text: SSH into the Gulf Coast portal container (password: nexus2026):
Navigate to the PKI directory and read the documentation:
Read the README left by the previous administrator:
Explore the directory structure:

whatThisDoes.concept: You’re mapping the PKI infrastructure before making changes. The CA/ directory holds the root Certificate Authority (ca.crt and ca.key). The certs/ directory holds the server certificates including the expired portal.crt. The csr/ directory is your working space for new certificate signing requests.
whatThisDoes.output: Note the file permissions — the CA key (ca.key) is root-owned for security, while the certs/ directory is student-owned so you can write new files there.

content.part: Part B: Generate a new Certificate Signing Request (CSR)

steps.text: Generate a new CSR using the existing private key:
steps.command: openssl req -new -key certs/portal.key -out csr/portal-new.csr -subj "/C=US/ST=Texas/L=Houston/O=Gulf Coast Healthcare Partners/CN=portal.gulfcoast.local"

whatThisDoes.concept: A Certificate Signing Request (CSR) is a formal application for a new certificate. It contains your public key and identity information, signed with your private key to prove ownership. The CA uses this to issue a new certificate without ever needing your private key.

flags.description: Use the existing private key (no need to regenerate)
Provides identity fields inline (C=country, ST=state, L=locality, O=organization, CN=common name)

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the CSR generation command and output. Save as gulfcoast-03-csr-generation.png

content.part: Part C: Sign the CSR with the Certificate Authority

steps.text: Sign the CSR using the CA certificate and key (requires sudo because the CA key is root-owned):
steps.command: sudo openssl x509 -req -in csr/portal-new.csr -CA CA/ca.crt -CAkey CA/ca.key -CAcreateserial -out certs/portal-new.crt -days 365 -sha256

whatThisDoes.concept: The CA “signs” your CSR by combining your identity and public key with the CA’s digital signature. Anyone who trusts the CA can now verify your certificate is legitimate. This is the chain of trust in action.
whatThisDoes.output: You’ll see “Certificate request self-signature ok” followed by the subject line. The sudo password is nexus2026.

flags.description: Process a CSR into a signed certificate
Specify the signing Certificate Authority and its key
Generate a unique serial number for this certificate
Use modern hashing algorithm for the signature

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the CA signing command and “self-signature ok” output. Save as gulfcoast-04-cert-signing.png

content.part: Part D: Install the new certificate

steps.text: Copy the new certificate into Apache’s SSL directory:

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the certificate installation command. Save as gulfcoast-05-cert-installation.png

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Wrong key path: The portal key is at certs/portal.key — confirm with ls -la
Permission denied on CA key: The CA private key is root-owned. You need sudo for the signing step
Subject mismatch: The CN (Common Name) must match the server hostname in the certificate

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: A new signed certificate installed in place of the expired one. Screenshots documenting the CSR generation, CA signing process, and certificate installation.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 7 explains the certificate signing process and chain of trust. The CSR-to-certificate workflow you perform mirrors real-world PKI operations.

content.whatYoureDoing: Restarting Apache to load the new certificate, verifying it works, and hunting for additional security findings.
content.whyItMatters: Verification proves the fix worked. Combined with your “before” screenshots, this creates a complete audit trail showing you diagnosed the problem, implemented a solution, and confirmed success.

content.part: Part A: Restart Apache and verify the fix

steps.text: Restart Apache to load the new certificate:

whatThisDoes.output: Restarting Apache inside the Docker container will close your SSH connection. You’ll see “Connection to localhost closed by remote host.” This is normal. The container may also stop.

steps.text: From your Kali terminal, restart the container if needed:

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the Apache restart and container restart. Save as gulfcoast-06-apache-restart.png

steps.text: From your Kali terminal (not inside the container), verify the new certificate dates:

whatThisDoes.output: The notAfter date should now be approximately one year in the future. Compare this with your “before” screenshot — the dates should be completely different.

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the new certificate dates showing future expiration. Save as gulfcoast-07-cert-valid.png

steps.text: Refresh https://localhost in Firefox — the expired certificate warning should be gone (you may still see a self-signed trust warning, which is a separate issue from expiration).

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the browser showing the portal without an expired certificate warning. Save as gulfcoast-08-browser-success.png

content.part: Part B: Bonus — Hunt for additional findings

steps.text: SSH back into the container and investigate the following areas. Document anything concerning in your report:
What permissions does the portal private key have? Are they appropriate?
What signature algorithm does the CA certificate use? Is it still considered secure?
When does the CA certificate itself expire?
What TLS protocol versions does the Apache configuration allow?

content.title: Bonus Discovery
content.text: SSH back in and investigate the areas listed above. Document anything concerning in your report. These bonus findings strengthen your deliverable and demonstrate thoroughness to the client.

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: SSH session disconnects on Apache restart — this is expected behavior inside Docker. Restart the container from your Kali terminal: sudo docker start gulfcoast-portal
Certificate change not reflected — if openssl still shows the old expiration date, run docker restart gulfcoast-portal and wait 10 seconds before checking again.

Self-signed trust warning persists in browser — this is separate from expiration. Your openssl verification is the authoritative check. The browser warning about self-signed CAs is expected and does not mean your fix failed.
Missing 'before' screenshots — you cannot recreate the broken state after fixing the certificate. If you missed them, note this limitation in your report.

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: A verified, working HTTPS connection with valid certificate dates. Screenshots showing Apache restart and successful verification via openssl. The “after” evidence combined with your “before” screenshots creates a complete remediation audit trail.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 7 discusses chain of trust verification. The browser warning about self-signed certificates is different from expiration — your openssl verification shows the fix worked even though the browser still flags the self-signed CA.

content.whatYoureDoing: Compiling all three weeks of Module 2 work into a professional Engagement Packet.
content.whyItMatters: This isn’t just an academic assignment — it’s a professional deliverable. In six weeks, Jennifer Walsh presents this documentation to OCR auditors as evidence of due diligence.

content.title: One Document, Three Sections
content.text: Your Engagement Packet is one .docx file containing all three sections in order. Do not submit separate files.

sections.label: Section A: NEXUS Timesheet
sections.description: A table showing your hours across all 3 weeks. Weeks 1–2: Internal training (not client billable) — Lab 2.1 and Lab 2.2 work. Week 3: Client billable hours — Gulf Coast certificate remediation. Include dates, activities, and hours per task.

sections.label: Section B: Internal Runbook
sections.description: “Here’s how to do this for the next analyst.” Cryptographic operations (from Lab 2.1), authentication systems and SSH configuration (from Lab 2.2), and certificate remediation workflow (from Lab 2.3). Commands, flags, expected outputs — enough that another analyst could reproduce your work.

sections.label: Section C: Client Report
sections.description: The professional deliverable for Jennifer Walsh. Uses the report structure defined below.

content.title: Report Structure (Section C)
items: Title Page
Executive Summary (1 page max, non-technical)
Table of Contents
Environment Specifications (from Lab 2.1)
Cryptographic Foundations (from Lab 2.1)
Authentication Systems (from Lab 2.2)
Certificate Remediation Findings (NEW)
Risk Analysis (NEW)
Recommendations (NEW)
Professional Reflection (NEW)
References (minimum 10)
Appendix

content.title: Executive Summary Tips
content.text: Write for Jennifer Walsh (CISO), not technical staff. Lead with finding and fix, not methodology.

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: PDF submission: Must be .docx — PDF submissions receive automatic zero
Missing VM name: Every screenshot must show your hostname (kali-lastname-studentID)
Fewer than 10 references: Man pages, RFCs, and textbook chapters all count

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: A complete Engagement Packet in .docx format containing all 12 required sections, 25+ screenshots, and minimum 10 references.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 11 covers compliance requirements for healthcare systems. Your remediation documentation demonstrates due diligence — exactly what HIPAA auditors expect.

redTeam.label: What Red Team Learns
items: Expired certificates are reconnaissance gold
Users trained to click through warnings make phishing easier
SHA-1 signatures reveal old infrastructure

blueTeam.label: What Blue Team Learns
items: Certificate lifecycle is a visibility problem
Defenders implement monitoring, automated renewal (ACME), and inventory management

purpleTeam.connection: You’re fixing an expired cert (blue) while hunting for additional findings (red). Jennifer Walsh hired NEXUS for someone who sees the whole picture.

items: All 12 required sections in correct order
File saved as .docx (NOT PDF)
Spell-checked and proofread
sections.title: Document Requirements

items: 25+ screenshots total (9 from Lab 2.1, 8 from Lab 2.2, 8+ from Lab 2.3)
VM hostname visible in every screenshot
sections.title: Screenshots

items: Minimum 10 references (man pages count!)
sections.title: References

canvasSubmission.text: Submit your completed Engagement Packet to the Lab 2.3 Assignment in Canvas as a single .docx file.

finalChecklist.finalWarning: PDF submissions receive an automatic zero. Double-check your file extension before submitting.

penalties.condition: Per missing reference below minimum of 10

gradingStandards.rubricCallout: Full rubric available in Canvas: IS3513_Assessment_Report_Rubric_v2.docx
gradingStandards.automaticZero: No submission, AI-generated content, fabricated screenshots, shared work, wrong format (PDF), or academic integrity violation.

customCallouts.title: Cleanup After Submitting
customCallouts.text:
After submitting, clean up the Docker container:
sudo docker stop gulfcoast-portal
sudo docker rm gulfcoast-portal

needHelp.nextModule: Module 3: Network Traffic Analysis — You’ll work with Gulf Coast again, analyzing network captures for anomalies.
needHelp.lateWorkPolicy: Late work is not accepted. Drop policies exist for emergencies.

permitted: Explain command syntax and tool functionality
prohibited: Complete hands-on exercises on your behalf
genAI.principle: AI is a tutor, not a ghostwriter.

whatsNext.text: Module 3: Network Traffic Analysis — You will work with Gulf Coast Healthcare again, this time analyzing packet captures for anomalous traffic patterns. The certificate infrastructure you fixed this week will appear in those captures as properly encrypted TLS sessions — demonstrating the before/after in network data.
